title: US launches 7th night of strikes near Strait of Hormuz
body:
The conflict between the United States and Iran has entered its 140th day, with both sides showing no signs of easing their attacks. The latest escalation has resulted in a significant disruption to maritime traffic, with the strategic Strait of Hormuz experiencing a virtual freeze. According to industry experts, the situation has become so dire that "nobody is willing to move" through the critical waterway, which is a vital conduit for global oil supplies. The ongoing strikes have created a sense of unease among shipping companies, with many opting to avoid the region altogether, fearing potential attacks or reprisals.

The latest round of strikes has been particularly intense, with the United States conducting its seventh consecutive night of attacks. The targets of these strikes have not been publicly disclosed, but it is believed that they are aimed at Iranian military installations and infrastructure. Meanwhile, Iran has continued to retaliate, with drone and rocket strikes reported in various parts of the region. On Friday, an Iranian Kurdish armed opposition group in Iraq's Kurdistan region came under attack, resulting in the loss of nine lives. The exiled party has blamed the attack on Iran, which has been accused of using force to suppress dissent and opposition in the region.

The Strait of Hormuz has become a focal point in the conflict, with both sides engaging in a game of cat and mouse. The waterway is a critical chokepoint, with a significant proportion of the world's oil passing through it. Any disruption to traffic in the region has the potential to send shockwaves through the global economy, making it a key strategic location for both the United States and Iran. The current freeze in traffic has raised concerns about the potential for an environmental disaster, with many ships carrying oil and other hazardous materials stuck in the region.

The ongoing conflict has also raised questions about the role of other regional players, including Iraq and the Kurdish opposition groups. The attack on the Iranian Kurdish armed opposition group has highlighted the complexities of the region, with multiple factions and interests at play. The United States has been accused of using these groups as proxies in its conflict with Iran, which has denied any involvement in the attack. As the situation continues to escalate, it is likely that other regional players will be drawn into the conflict, further complicating the already fraught landscape.

The international community has been watching the situation with growing concern, with many calling for a de-escalation of tensions. The United Nations has urged both sides to engage in dialogue and find a peaceful resolution to the conflict. However, with both sides dug in and refusing to back down, it is unclear what the future holds. The current situation has the potential to spiral out of control, with devastating consequences for the region and the global economy.

As the conflict enters its fifth month, it is clear that there is no end in sight. The United States and Iran remain locked in a standoff, with neither side willing to give in. The situation in the Strait of Hormuz continues to deteriorate, with shipping traffic at a standstill. As the world waits with bated breath, one thing is certain - the consequences of this conflict will be far-reaching and profound. The question on everyone's mind is what the future holds, and whether the two sides can find a way to resolve their differences before it's too late.
